name: The Passé Composé
summary: The everyday past tense, built with avoir or être plus a past participle.
explanation:
The passé composé is how French talks about completed past actions. It has two parts: a helper verb (avoir or être) in the present, plus a past participle. J'ai mangé (I ate / I have eaten).

Most verbs use avoir: j'ai parlé, tu as fini, il a vu. A small set of movement and change-of-state verbs use être: je suis allé (I went), elle est partie (she left). With être, the participle agrees with the subject, adding -e for feminine and -s for plural: elle est allée.

Regular participles follow patterns by verb group: -er verbs give -é (parlé), -ir verbs give -i (fini), -re verbs give -u (vendu). Many common verbs are irregular: avoir gives eu, être gives été, faire gives fait.

conjugation.être verbs: movement / change of state (aller, venir, partir, naître, mourir)

commonMistakes: Choosing the wrong helper. Movement and change-of-state verbs take être, not avoir.
Forgetting agreement with être: it is elle est allée, not elle est allé.
Using the wrong participle ending: -er gives -é, -ir gives -i, -re gives -u.
Regularising irregular participles: avoir is eu, être is été, faire is fait.
Leaving out the helper verb. You always need avoir or être plus the participle.
tip: Ask: did the subject move from A to B or change state? If yes, use être (with agreement). Otherwise use avoir.
